title: Best Weighted Blanket 2026: Five Options Compared on Fill, Weight, and Real-World Feel
description: From a $55 YnM glass bead blanket to a $249 Bearaby chunky-knit. Five weighted blankets compared on fill type, actual weight distribution, heat retention, washability, and who genuinely benefits from each.
lede: Weighted blankets work for some people and are overpriced dead weight for others. The difference comes down to fill type (glass beads versus knit weight), whether the weight is evenly distributed, how hot it sleeps, and whether the cover survives regular washing. Five options from ¥5,990 to $249 compared here.
methodology: We assessed each blanket on fill construction and weight distribution, heat retention characteristics, cover washability, and long-term durability signals from aggregated owner reviews. Specifications were verified against manufacturer product pages as of May 2026.

recommendedFor.label: For anxiety relief and sensory input during sleep
recommendedFor.reason: The original — 7-layer glass bead construction delivers consistent, even deep-pressure stimulation across the body that lighter blankets approximate but don't replicate.

recommendedFor.label: For hot sleepers who want weight without overheating
recommendedFor.reason: Open-weave chunky knit construction has no trapped-air pockets — the most breathable weighted option in this comparison by a significant margin.

recommendedFor.label: For budget-first buyers testing weighted blankets for the first time
recommendedFor.reason: At $55–$89, it's low enough to lose nothing if weighted blankets turn out not to work for you, and the 7-layer construction is legitimate for the price.

recommendedFor.label: For Japanese market buyers who want domestic availability
recommendedFor.reason: Available at 500+ Nitori stores nationwide, no import or customs risk, sizes match Japanese JIS single standard, and the price is accessible at ¥5,990–9,990.

recommendedFor.label: For couples sharing a bed who want OEKO-TEX certified materials
recommendedFor.reason: OEKO-TEX certification covers both the cover and the glass beads, the dual-sided texture suits different seasonal preferences, and the 7-layer construction distributes weight consistently.

sections.heading: How we compared
paragraphs: Weighted blankets are measured in two ways that matter: fill weight accuracy (does the blanket actually weigh what it says on the label, and is that weight evenly distributed across the body surface) and heat retention (how much does the fill and cover construction trap body heat). We did not run controlled thermal imaging or independent weight distribution tests. What we did: verify stated weights against manufacturer specifications, cross-reference owner review patterns across Amazon US, Amazon Japan, and brand direct pages, and check cover fabric certifications (OEKO-TEX, GOTS) against the issuing body registries.

The standard recommendation of 'choose 10% of your body weight' is a starting point, not a prescription. A 60 kg adult using a 6 kg blanket is the textbook pairing; in practice, preference varies. Heavier sleepers who prefer full-body coverage and lighter sleepers who want the weight concentrated on the torso often deviate from the 10% guideline. The five options in this comparison cover 3 kg to 11.3 kg — the range where most adults find weighted blankets usable.

One genuine limitation: we compared blankets across Japanese (single 150x200 cm) and US/international sizing, and the size mismatch matters. A US 'throw' (48x72 inches, 122x183 cm) does not cover a full single-size Japanese bed; a US 'twin' (60x80 inches, 152x203 cm) does. Buyers in Japan who want full-bed coverage should verify dimensions before purchasing US-import weighted blankets.

sections.heading: What changed in 2026
paragraphs: The weighted blanket category matured. The 2019–2021 wave of nearly identical glass-bead-in-polyester-cover blankets flooded Amazon and drove prices to commodity levels — YnM, Degrees of Comfort, and several other brands now provide functionally equivalent 7-layer glass bead construction at $50–$90, making the $150+ premium options justify their price on specific differentiators rather than basic function.

Bearaby's knit-weight design — where the blanket weight comes from the yarn mass rather than bead fill — found a durable niche that the commodity wave did not replicate. The chunky-knit construction is visually distinctive (a meaningful factor for buyers who care about bedroom aesthetics) and genuinely cooler than any glass-bead blanket at equivalent weight, because the open weave allows airflow that bead fill with a solid cover prevents. The trade-off is bulkiness and the inability to machine wash the full blanket without a front-load washer rated for heavy loads.

In Japan, the category arrived later than in the US and is still in early growth. Nitori's entry at ¥5,990–9,990 brought accessible domestic pricing, but the selection is limited (two weights, one or two colorways) compared to US-market depth. MUJI does not currently offer a weighted blanket in their standard lineup. The Japanese market buyer who wants more options still primarily imports from US brands, accepting customs fees and sizing differences as the cost.

sections.heading: Where each fits
paragraphs: Gravity Blanket at $189–$249 is the category originator. The 7-layer construction — glass micro-beads distributed in 6-inch square compartments across the blanket, sandwiched between a quilted duvet insert and a removable cover — was the reference design that most competitors copied. The specific claim Gravity makes is consistent weight distribution without bead shifting: the compartment grid prevents beads from migrating to blanket corners under use. Owner reviews at 5+ year ownership confirm this holds; no significant bead-clustering reports appear in the long-term review cohort. The honest weakness is heat — the polyester fill cover is not breathable by design, and owner reviews include a consistent 'warm in summer' signal that no cover swap fully eliminates. At $249 for the 20 lb option, it's the highest price in this comparison for standard bead-fill construction.

Bearaby Cotton Napper at $249–$299 uses a fundamentally different approach. The weight comes from GOTS-certified organic cotton yarn knitted into a chunky open-weave construction — no glass beads, no fill pockets, just the mass of the yarn distributed across the blanket. The open weave is the key differentiator: airflow passes through the blanket, making it the only genuinely cool-sleeping weighted option in this comparison. The trade-offs are real: the chunky knit looks visually large on a bed (takes up more visual space than a flat blanket), weighs more than its fill-weight equivalent because of the yarn structure, and requires a front-load washer for the 20 lb and 25 lb options — a top-load agitator will damage the knit. For buyers who sleep hot, the Bearaby is the only weighted blanket in this comparison that does not compound the heat problem.

YnM at $55–$89 is the commodity-tier reference. The 7-layer glass bead construction is functionally comparable to Gravity at roughly one-third the price — the difference is in cover material quality (YnM's standard option is 100% cotton, comparable to Gravity's; the premium options add cooling fabric at modest upcharge) and long-term owner data depth. YnM has 8+ years of Amazon reviews providing a larger sample than newer entrants. The structural weakness: bead distribution consistency is slightly less uniform than Gravity's compartment grid at the extreme edges of the blanket, leading to occasional 'beads shift toward one side' reports in long-term use. This is a minor issue for most uses but worth knowing if you move significantly in sleep.

Nitori's weighted blanket at ¥5,990–9,990 is the Japan-market practical choice. Available at 500+ Nitori stores nationwide with no import risk, the 3 kg and 5 kg options in standard Japanese single sizing (150x200 cm) cover the range where most Japanese adults (50–70 kg body weight, targeting 5–7% of body weight) find weighted blankets comfortable. The cover is polyester rather than cotton, which retains more heat than Gravity or YnM's cotton options — a relevant difference in Japanese summers. Nitori does not publish fill weight per compartment or compartment size, which makes detailed construction comparison difficult. For buyers who want domestic availability and after-sale support without import complexity, Nitori is the sensible default.

Luna at $65–$95 is the OEKO-TEX-certified mid-tier option. The certification covers the glass beads and the cover fabric — a meaningful distinction from YnM, which certifies the cover but not the bead fill. The dual-sided cover (one side plush minky, one side cotton) serves seasonal switching without buying a separate cover. The 7-layer glass bead construction is comparable to YnM and Gravity in design; at $65–$95 it sits between YnM and Gravity on price. The honest gap versus Gravity: Gravity's long-term owner review depth (8+ years on Amazon) is larger than Luna's, making multi-year durability confidence slightly lower for Luna despite equivalent certification.

sections.heading: Verdict
paragraphs: For first-time weighted blanket buyers who don't know yet whether the format works for them: YnM at $55–$89 is the right entry point. The 7-layer glass bead construction is legitimate, the price is low enough that if weighted blankets turn out not to suit your sleep, the loss is minor, and 8+ years of Amazon reviews give a real durability signal. In Japan, Nitori's ¥5,990–9,990 option is the equivalent — domestic availability without import friction, accessible price, standard Japanese sizing.

For hot sleepers who know they want weighted pressure but cannot tolerate additional heat: Bearaby Cotton Napper is the only option in this comparison that solves both problems. The open-weave knit is genuinely breathable; no glass-bead blanket in this comparison matches it on heat dissipation. Accept the higher price ($249–$299), the machine-wash requirement for a front-load washer, and the visual bulk of a chunky-knit blanket as the trade-offs. For buyers who want the longest-track-record bead-fill construction at premium quality: Gravity Blanket at $189–$249 is the original and the benchmark.

products.review: Gravity is the reference design — 6-inch square compartment grid prevents bead migration, 7-layer construction has 8+ years of owner-verified durability, and the 15 lb (6.8 kg) and 20 lb (9.1 kg) weights cover the range most adults use. Owner reviews at 5+ year use show no significant bead-clustering or seam failure patterns. The honest weakness is heat: the standard polyester cover retains body heat perceptibly, and summer use without air conditioning sleeps warm. The premium minky or cooling cover options reduce this somewhat but do not eliminate it. At $249 for the 20 lb, it's the highest price for standard glass-bead construction in this comparison.
pros: Compartment grid prevents bead shifting to edges
8+ years of owner-verified durability on Amazon
15 lb and 20 lb options cover the widest weight range
Removable cover swappable for different textures
cons: Standard cover retains heat in warm bedrooms
Highest price for glass-bead construction in this comparison

products.review: Bearaby solves the heat problem that every glass-bead blanket has but few honestly address. The open-weave chunky knit allows airflow through the blanket surface — no trapped air pockets, no continuous fabric layer blocking heat dissipation. GOTS-certified organic cotton is the only material used, making it the cleanest-certified option in this comparison. The 20 lb (9.1 kg) and 25 lb (11.3 kg) weights require a front-load washer for home washing — top-load agitators damage the knit. The visual footprint on a bed is large; the chunky knit reads as a lifestyle statement as much as a sleep product. For buyers who sleep hot and want weighted pressure: this is the only option in this comparison that does not make the heat problem worse.
pros: Open-weave knit dissipates heat — genuinely breathable
GOTS-certified organic cotton throughout
No glass beads — no shifting, no seam leaks
Visually distinctive — doubles as bedroom decor
cons: 20 lb and 25 lb require front-load washer
Bulkier footprint on the bed than flat blankets

specs.Wash: Machine wash cold, front-load for 20+ lb

products.review: YnM is the commodity-tier benchmark — 7-layer glass bead construction for $55–$89 that covers the same basic function as Gravity at roughly one-third the price. The 100% cotton cover on the standard option is comparable in breathability to Gravity's standard cover; the premium cooling-fabric cover option adds modest heat reduction at modest upcharge. The structural caveat: bead distribution at the extreme edges is slightly less uniform than Gravity's rigid compartment grid, leading to occasional bead-shifting reports in long-term use from restless sleepers. For buyers trying weighted blankets for the first time at minimal financial risk, or buyers who confirmed weighted blankets work for them and want a second blanket without spending premium pricing: YnM is the honest recommendation.
pros: One-third the price of Gravity for equivalent function
8+ years of Amazon reviews provide real durability data
Available from 5 lb to 25 lb — widest weight range in comparison
Multiple cover fabric options including cooling
cons: Bead distribution at edges slightly less uniform than Gravity

products.review: Nitori's weighted blanket is the practical Japan-market answer — 500+ stores nationwide, standard Japanese single sizing (150x200 cm), 3 kg and 5 kg options that cover the weight range suitable for most Japanese adults in the 50–70 kg body weight range at 5–7% of body weight. The polyester cover retains heat more than cotton alternatives, which is a meaningful factor in Japanese summer bedrooms without air conditioning. Nitori does not publish compartment grid size or fill weight per compartment, making precise construction comparison with US imports difficult. The after-sale reality: returns and exchanges through Nitori's store network are straightforward; returns on US-import weighted blankets require international shipping at buyer cost.
pros: Available at 500+ Nitori stores — no import, no customs
Standard Japanese single sizing (150x200 cm)
¥5,990–9,990 pricing accessible for Japan market
Straightforward returns through store network
cons: Polyester cover retains more heat than cotton alternatives
Construction details (compartment size, fill density) not published
Only 3 kg and 5 kg — narrower weight range than US imports

products.review: Luna's OEKO-TEX certification covers both the glass bead fill and the cover fabric — a distinction that matters for buyers with chemical sensitivity or who prioritize certified materials. The dual-sided cover (plush minky on one side, cotton on the other) serves seasonal switching without buying a separate cover: cotton side in summer for lower heat retention, minky side in winter for warmth. The 7-layer construction is equivalent to YnM and Gravity in design. At $65–$95, it sits between YnM and Gravity on price. The honest gap versus Gravity: Luna's owner review history is shorter (5–6 years versus Gravity's 8+), so multi-year durability confidence, while positive in available data, has a smaller sample behind it.
pros: OEKO-TEX certification covers beads and cover fabric
Dual-sided cover for seasonal switching
Mid-tier pricing between YnM and Gravity
12 lb to 25 lb weight range
cons: Shorter owner review history than Gravity
Plush minky side adds heat retention in warm bedrooms

faqs.q: Do weighted blankets actually help with sleep or anxiety?
faqs.a: The clinical evidence is limited but directionally positive for specific populations. A 2020 study in the Journal of Clinical Sleep Medicine (Ong et al.) found statistically significant reductions in insomnia severity in adults with chronic insomnia who used a weighted blanket versus a light blanket over four weeks. Studies in autism spectrum disorder populations show consistent reduction in self-reported anxiety with weighted blanket use. The mechanism proposed is deep pressure stimulation — similar to the calming effect of a firm hug — which increases serotonin and reduces cortisol. The honest framing: weighted blankets work for some people, do nothing for others, and a small subset finds them claustrophobic or hot enough to worsen sleep. The low-price-entry strategy (try YnM at $55–$89 before committing to Gravity at $249) exists for this reason.

The '10% of body weight' rule is the starting point most brands use. A 60 kg adult would target a 6 kg (about 13 lb) blanket; in practice, the nearest available option (5 kg / 11 lb from Nitori, or 15 lb from US brands) is close enough. Buyers who want full-body coverage tend toward higher weights; buyers who use the blanket primarily on the torso can use lower weights. Side sleepers sometimes find heavy blankets restrict shoulder movement — if you sleep on your side primarily, consider starting at the lower end of the weight range. Children should use lighter weights than the 10% rule suggests; weighted blankets below 2 kg are not recommended for children under 5 due to suffocation risk.

faqs.q: Can weighted blankets be machine washed?
faqs.a: Most glass-bead weighted blankets up to 15 lb (6.8 kg) can be machine washed in a large-capacity front-load washer on a gentle cold cycle. The beads themselves are not damaged by water; the risk is the seam stitching under the mechanical stress of an agitator cycle. Top-load washers with agitators are risky for weighted blankets above 12 lb — the agitator can stress the compartment stitching and cause seam failure over repeated washes. Commercial laundromats with front-load machines (20–30 lb capacity) handle 20 lb blankets safely. Bearaby's knit construction requires front-load wash for the 20 lb and 25 lb options specifically due to knit deformation risk from top-load agitators. Always check the care label — some polyester covers are dry-clean only.

pinDescription: Five weighted blankets compared in 2026 — from a ¥5,990 Nitori to a $299 Bearaby chunky knit. Fill type, heat retention, washability, and who actually benefits from each weight explained honestly.
